{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}
former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名 = Shibukawa Tenjin
Sobukawa Tenjin
Inba Tenjin
Tenjin |札所等 = |例祭 = Sunday nearest October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Shibukawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Shibukawa Shrine''' (しぶかわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Inba Motomachi|Q86729809|WD=Q86729809}}, {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} identified with "Shibukawa Shrine" of Yamada District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506}}, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}. In earlier times, "Shibukawa" was read "Sobukawa," and it is said to have attracted faith as the {{ill|lt=sōsha|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129}} of Yamada District, Owari Province. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN|WD=|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} (たかみむすびのかみ) === History === Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN}}, it was called '''Tenjin'''. The 17th-century ''Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki'' records "Inba Village... Sobukawa Tenjin... exempt from levies from before," showing that it was already called "Sobukawa Tenjin" in the early Edo period. According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}}, and in Hakuhō 5 ({{ill|lt=676|Q31098|WD=Q31098}}) it became the {{ill|lt=saijō|Q12042092|WD=Q12042092}} (yuniwa) for the {{ill|lt=Daijōsai|Q11433653|WD=Q11433653}} held in Yamada District, Owari Province. At founding, it is thought to have been at a site southwest of the current location called "Shibuko" (sobuko). The {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}} does mention that Yamada District in Owari was chosen as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yuki|1=UNKNOWN}} district for the {{ill|lt=Niinamesai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518}} in 676, but does not mention Shibukawa Shrine. It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030}} with "Shibukawa Shrine" in Yamada District, Owari, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and this became the accepted theory. Because the shrine and place names are similar, there has been almost no dissent from identifying this as Shibukawa Shrine, with only {{ill|lt=Tsuda Masao|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474}} raising doubts. For divine rank ({{ill|lt=shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}}), the Teiji manuscript of the {{ill|lt=Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}} lists "Junior Third Rank Upper, Shibukawa Tenjin," while the Genki manuscript has "Junior Third Rank, Shibukawa Tenjin." In the Sengoku period, the shrine buildings were renovated by {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411}}. In Jōkyō 5 (1688), they were rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245}}, lord of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}}. Major repairs were carried out in Kansei 3 (1791) and in 1898 (Meiji 31). In September 1959 (Shōwa 34), the {{ill|lt=Isewan Typhoon|Q1060156|WD=Q1060156}} toppled dozens of giant cypress trees over 300 years old. After that, roofs were rethatched every 33 to 34 years and shrine buildings restored every 100 years. In {{ill|lt=1897|Q7847|WD=Q7847}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 30), nearby Hakken-sha and Yamagami were merged into this shrine. Because these shrine structures were the oldest buildings in {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}, the city took a positive stance toward cultural-property designation and consulted its cultural property protection council. A survey by Chubu University engineering professor {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ono Takehisa|1=UNKNOWN}} was scheduled for May 12, 2002, but on May 4, eight days before the survey, a suspicious fire burned the shrine buildings to the ground. A temporary shrine was built in June that year, and the Shibukawa Shrine Reconstruction Committee was formed to plan remains surveys and rebuilding''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.694. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in May 2009 (Heisei 21). Construction cost exceeded 240 million yen, and more than 85 million yen in donations were collected. === Precincts === ; Buildings * Honden - {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}, cypress-bark roofing * Haiden * Temizuya * Assembly hall * Yuki-saiden monument ; Subsidiary shrines * East Gosha-no-miya * West Gosha-no-miya * Shinmei-sha * Kumano-sha * Hachiman-sha * Imake-sha * Yamagami-sha * Hakken-sha * Inari-sha * Tsushima-sha === Local information === ; Location * 488-0840 {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}Inba Motomachi 5-3-1 ; Access * 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Seto Line|Q6810422|WD=Q6810422}}{{ill|lt=Inba Station|Q8192040|WD=Q8192040}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.696 }} === References === * ''Owariasahi City History, Cultural Properties Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980 * ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 2011 === External links === * [https://sbkw.o.oo7.jp/index2.html Official website] {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しふかわしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:尾張旭市の歴史]] [[Category:尾張旭市の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}
former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名 = Shibukawa Tenjin
Sobukawa Tenjin
Inba Tenjin
Tenjin |札所等 = |例祭 = Sunday nearest October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Shibukawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Shibukawa Shrine''' (しぶかわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Inba Motomachi|Q86729809|WD=Q86729809}}, {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} identified with "Shibukawa Shrine" of Yamada District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506}}, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}. In earlier times, "Shibukawa" was read "Sobukawa," and it is said to have attracted faith as the {{ill|lt=sōsha|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129}} of Yamada District, Owari Province. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} (たかみむすびのかみ) === History === Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN}}, it was called '''Tenjin'''. The 17th-century ''Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki'' records "Inba Village... Sobukawa Tenjin... exempt from levies from before," showing that it was already called "Sobukawa Tenjin" in the early Edo period. According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}}, and in Hakuhō 5 ({{ill|lt=676|Q31098|WD=Q31098}}) it became the {{ill|lt=saijō|Q12042092|WD=Q12042092}} (yuniwa) for the {{ill|lt=Daijōsai|Q11433653|WD=Q11433653}} held in Yamada District, Owari Province. At founding, it is thought to have been at a site southwest of the current location called "Shibuko" (sobuko). The {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}} does mention that Yamada District in Owari was chosen as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yuki|1=UNKNOWN}} district for the {{ill|lt=Niinamesai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518}} in 676, but does not mention Shibukawa Shrine. It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030}} with "Shibukawa Shrine" in Yamada District, Owari, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and this became the accepted theory. Because the shrine and place names are similar, there has been almost no dissent from identifying this as Shibukawa Shrine, with only {{ill|lt=Tsuda Masao|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474}} raising doubts. For divine rank ({{ill|lt=shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}}), the Teiji manuscript of the {{ill|lt=Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}} lists "Junior Third Rank Upper, Shibukawa Tenjin," while the Genki manuscript has "Junior Third Rank, Shibukawa Tenjin." In the Sengoku period, the shrine buildings were renovated by {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411}}. In Jōkyō 5 (1688), they were rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245}}, lord of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}}. Major repairs were carried out in Kansei 3 (1791) and in 1898 (Meiji 31). In September 1959 (Shōwa 34), the {{ill|lt=Isewan Typhoon|Q1060156|WD=Q1060156}} toppled dozens of giant cypress trees over 300 years old. After that, roofs were rethatched every 33 to 34 years and shrine buildings restored every 100 years. In {{ill|lt=1897|Q7847|WD=Q7847}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 30), nearby Hakken-sha and Yamagami were merged into this shrine. Because these shrine structures were the oldest buildings in {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}, the city took a positive stance toward cultural-property designation and consulted its cultural property protection council. A survey by Chubu University engineering professor {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ono Takehisa|1=UNKNOWN}} was scheduled for May 12, 2002, but on May 4, eight days before the survey, a suspicious fire burned the shrine buildings to the ground. A temporary shrine was built in June that year, and the Shibukawa Shrine Reconstruction Committee was formed to plan remains surveys and rebuilding''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.694. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in May 2009 (Heisei 21). Construction cost exceeded 240 million yen, and more than 85 million yen in donations were collected. === Precincts === ; Buildings * Honden - {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}, cypress-bark roofing * Haiden * Temizuya * Assembly hall * Yuki-saiden monument ; Subsidiary shrines * East Gosha-no-miya * West Gosha-no-miya * Shinmei-sha * Kumano-sha * Hachiman-sha * Imake-sha * Yamagami-sha * Hakken-sha * Inari-sha * Tsushima-sha === Local information === ; Location * 488-0840 {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}Inba Motomachi 5-3-1 ; Access * 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Seto Line|Q6810422|WD=Q6810422}}{{ill|lt=Inba Station|Q8192040|WD=Q8192040}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.696 }} === References === * ''Owariasahi City History, Cultural Properties Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980 * ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 2011 === External links === * [https://sbkw.o.oo7.jp/index2.html Official website] {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しふかわしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:尾張旭市の歴史]] [[Category:尾張旭市の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}{{ill|lt=Inba Motomachi|Q86729809|WD=Q86729809}}5-3-1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|lt=Junior Third Rank|Q11071123|WD=Q11071123}} upper
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}
former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名 = Shibukawa Tenjin
Sobukawa Tenjin
Inba Tenjin
Tenjin |札所等 = |例祭 = Sunday nearest October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Shibukawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Shibukawa Shrine''' (しぶかわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Inba Motomachi|Q86729809|WD=Q86729809}}, {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} identified with "Shibukawa Shrine" of Yamada District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506}}, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}. In earlier times, "Shibukawa" was read "Sobukawa," and it is said to have attracted faith as the {{ill|lt=sōsha|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129}} of Yamada District, Owari Province. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} (たかみむすびのかみ) === History === Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN}}, it was called '''Tenjin'''. The 17th-century ''Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki'' records "Inba Village... Sobukawa Tenjin... exempt from levies from before," showing that it was already called "Sobukawa Tenjin" in the early Edo period. According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}}, and in Hakuhō 5 ({{ill|lt=676|Q31098|WD=Q31098}}) it became the {{ill|lt=saijō|Q12042092|WD=Q12042092}} (yuniwa) for the {{ill|lt=Daijōsai|Q11433653|WD=Q11433653}} held in Yamada District, Owari Province. At founding, it is thought to have been at a site southwest of the current location called "Shibuko" (sobuko). The {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}} does mention that Yamada District in Owari was chosen as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yuki|1=UNKNOWN}} district for the {{ill|lt=Niinamesai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518}} in 676, but does not mention Shibukawa Shrine. It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030}} with "Shibukawa Shrine" in Yamada District, Owari, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and this became the accepted theory. Because the shrine and place names are similar, there has been almost no dissent from identifying this as Shibukawa Shrine, with only {{ill|lt=Tsuda Masao|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474}} raising doubts. For divine rank ({{ill|lt=shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}}), the Teiji manuscript of the {{ill|lt=Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}} lists "Junior Third Rank Upper, Shibukawa Tenjin," while the Genki manuscript has "Junior Third Rank, Shibukawa Tenjin." In the Sengoku period, the shrine buildings were renovated by {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411}}. In Jōkyō 5 (1688), they were rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245}}, lord of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}}. Major repairs were carried out in Kansei 3 (1791) and in 1898 (Meiji 31). In September 1959 (Shōwa 34), the {{ill|lt=Isewan Typhoon|Q1060156|WD=Q1060156}} toppled dozens of giant cypress trees over 300 years old. After that, roofs were rethatched every 33 to 34 years and shrine buildings restored every 100 years. In {{ill|lt=1897|Q7847|WD=Q7847}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 30), nearby Hakken-sha and Yamagami were merged into this shrine. Because these shrine structures were the oldest buildings in {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}, the city took a positive stance toward cultural-property designation and consulted its cultural property protection council. A survey by Chubu University engineering professor {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ono Takehisa|1=UNKNOWN}} was scheduled for May 12, 2002, but on May 4, eight days before the survey, a suspicious fire burned the shrine buildings to the ground. A temporary shrine was built in June that year, and the Shibukawa Shrine Reconstruction Committee was formed to plan remains surveys and rebuilding''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.694. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in May 2009 (Heisei 21). Construction cost exceeded 240 million yen, and more than 85 million yen in donations were collected. === Precincts === ; Buildings * Honden - {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}, cypress-bark roofing * Haiden * Temizuya * Assembly hall * Yuki-saiden monument ; Subsidiary shrines * East Gosha-no-miya * West Gosha-no-miya * Shinmei-sha * Kumano-sha * Hachiman-sha * Imake-sha * Yamagami-sha * Hakken-sha * Inari-sha * Tsushima-sha === Local information === ; Location * 488-0840 {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}Inba Motomachi 5-3-1 ; Access * 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Seto Line|Q6810422|WD=Q6810422}}{{ill|lt=Inba Station|Q8192040|WD=Q8192040}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.696 }} === References === * ''Owariasahi City History, Cultural Properties Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980 * ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 2011 === External links === * [https://sbkw.o.oo7.jp/index2.html Official website] {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しふかわしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:尾張旭市の歴史]] [[Category:尾張旭市の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}{{ill|lt=Inba Motomachi|Q86729809|WD=Q86729809}}5-3-1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|lt=Junior Third Rank|Q11071123|WD=Q11071123}} upper
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}
former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名 = Shibukawa Tenjin
Sobukawa Tenjin
Inba Tenjin
Tenjin |札所等 = |例祭 = Sunday nearest October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Shibukawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Shibukawa Shrine''' (しぶかわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Inba Motomachi|Q86729809|WD=Q86729809}}, {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} identified with "Shibukawa Shrine" of Yamada District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506}}, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}. In earlier times, "Shibukawa" was read "Sobukawa," and it is said to have attracted faith as the {{ill|lt=sōsha|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129}} of Yamada District, Owari Province. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} (たかみむすびのかみ) === History === Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q184963}}, it was called '''Tenjin'''. The 17th-century ''Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki'' records "Inba Village... Sobukawa Tenjin... exempt from levies from before," showing that it was already called "Sobukawa Tenjin" in the early Edo period. According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}}, and in Hakuhō 5 ({{ill|lt=676|Q31098|WD=Q31098}}) it became the {{ill|lt=saijō|Q12042092|WD=Q12042092}} (yuniwa) for the {{ill|lt=Daijōsai|Q11433653|WD=Q11433653}} held in Yamada District, Owari Province. At founding, it is thought to have been at a site southwest of the current location called "Shibuko" (sobuko). The {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}} does mention that Yamada District in Owari was chosen as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yuki|1=UNKNOWN}} district for the {{ill|lt=Niinamesai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518}} in 676, but does not mention Shibukawa Shrine. It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030}} with "Shibukawa Shrine" in Yamada District, Owari, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and this became the accepted theory. Because the shrine and place names are similar, there has been almost no dissent from identifying this as Shibukawa Shrine, with only {{ill|lt=Tsuda Masao|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474}} raising doubts. For divine rank ({{ill|lt=shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}}), the Teiji manuscript of the {{ill|lt=Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}} lists "Junior Third Rank Upper, Shibukawa Tenjin," while the Genki manuscript has "Junior Third Rank, Shibukawa Tenjin." In the Sengoku period, the shrine buildings were renovated by {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411}}. In Jōkyō 5 (1688), they were rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245}}, lord of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}}. Major repairs were carried out in Kansei 3 (1791) and in 1898 (Meiji 31). In September 1959 (Shōwa 34), the {{ill|lt=Isewan Typhoon|Q1060156|WD=Q1060156}} toppled dozens of giant cypress trees over 300 years old. After that, roofs were rethatched every 33 to 34 years and shrine buildings restored every 100 years. In {{ill|lt=1897|Q7847|WD=Q7847}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 30), nearby Hakken-sha and Yamagami were merged into this shrine. Because these shrine structures were the oldest buildings in {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}, the city took a positive stance toward cultural-property designation and consulted its cultural property protection council. A survey by Chubu University engineering professor {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ono Takehisa|1=UNKNOWN}} was scheduled for May 12, 2002, but on May 4, eight days before the survey, a suspicious fire burned the shrine buildings to the ground. A temporary shrine was built in June that year, and the Shibukawa Shrine Reconstruction Committee was formed to plan remains surveys and rebuilding''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.694. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in May 2009 (Heisei 21). Construction cost exceeded 240 million yen, and more than 85 million yen in donations were collected. === Precincts === ; Buildings * Honden - {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}, cypress-bark roofing * Haiden * Temizuya * Assembly hall * Yuki-saiden monument ; Subsidiary shrines * East Gosha-no-miya * West Gosha-no-miya * Shinmei-sha * Kumano-sha * Hachiman-sha * Imake-sha * Yamagami-sha * Hakken-sha * Inari-sha * Tsushima-sha === Local information === ; Location * 488-0840 {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}Inba Motomachi 5-3-1 ; Access * 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Seto Line|Q6810422|WD=Q6810422}}{{ill|lt=Inba Station|Q8192040|WD=Q8192040}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.696 }} === References === * ''Owariasahi City History, Cultural Properties Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980 * ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 2011 === External links === * [https://sbkw.o.oo7.jp/index2.html Official website] {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しふかわしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:尾張旭市の歴史]] [[Category:尾張旭市の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名 = Shibukawa Tenjin
Sobukawa Tenjin
Inba Tenjin
Tenjin |札所等 = |例祭 = Sunday nearest October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Shibukawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Shibukawa Shrine''' (しぶかわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Inba Motomachi|Q86729809|WD=Q86729809}}, {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} identified with "Shibukawa Shrine" of Yamada District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506}}, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}. In earlier times, "Shibukawa" was read "Sobukawa," and it is said to have attracted faith as the {{ill|lt=sōsha|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129}} of Yamada District, Owari Province. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} (たかみむすびのかみ) === History === Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q184963}}, it was called '''Tenjin'''. The 17th-century ''Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki'' records "Inba Village... Sobukawa Tenjin... exempt from levies from before," showing that it was already called "Sobukawa Tenjin" in the early Edo period. According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}}, and in Hakuhō 5 ({{ill|lt=676|Q31098|WD=Q31098}}) it became the {{ill|lt=saijō|Q12042092|WD=Q12042092}} (yuniwa) for the {{ill|lt=Daijōsai|Q11433653|WD=Q11433653}} held in Yamada District, Owari Province. At founding, it is thought to have been at a site southwest of the current location called "Shibuko" (sobuko). The {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}} does mention that Yamada District in Owari was chosen as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yuki|1=UNKNOWN}} district for the {{ill|lt=Niinamesai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518}} in 676, but does not mention Shibukawa Shrine. It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030}} with "Shibukawa Shrine" in Yamada District, Owari, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and this became the accepted theory. Because the shrine and place names are similar, there has been almost no dissent from identifying this as Shibukawa Shrine, with only {{ill|lt=Tsuda Masao|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474}} raising doubts. For divine rank ({{ill|lt=shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}}), the Teiji manuscript of the {{ill|lt=Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}} lists "Junior Third Rank Upper, Shibukawa Tenjin," while the Genki manuscript has "Junior Third Rank, Shibukawa Tenjin." In the Sengoku period, the shrine buildings were renovated by {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411}}. In Jōkyō 5 (1688), they were rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245}}, lord of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}}. Major repairs were carried out in Kansei 3 (1791) and in 1898 (Meiji 31). In September 1959 (Shōwa 34), the {{ill|lt=Isewan Typhoon|Q1060156|WD=Q1060156}} toppled dozens of giant cypress trees over 300 years old. After that, roofs were rethatched every 33 to 34 years and shrine buildings restored every 100 years. In {{ill|lt=1897|Q7847|WD=Q7847}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 30), nearby Hakken-sha and Yamagami were merged into this shrine. Because these shrine structures were the oldest buildings in {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}, the city took a positive stance toward cultural-property designation and consulted its cultural property protection council. A survey by Chubu University engineering professor {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ono Takehisa|1=UNKNOWN}} was scheduled for May 12, 2002, but on May 4, eight days before the survey, a suspicious fire burned the shrine buildings to the ground. A temporary shrine was built in June that year, and the Shibukawa Shrine Reconstruction Committee was formed to plan remains surveys and rebuilding''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.694. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in May 2009 (Heisei 21). Construction cost exceeded 240 million yen, and more than 85 million yen in donations were collected. === Precincts === ; Buildings * Honden - {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}, cypress-bark roofing * Haiden * Temizuya * Assembly hall * Yuki-saiden monument ; Subsidiary shrines * East Gosha-no-miya * West Gosha-no-miya * Shinmei-sha * Kumano-sha * Hachiman-sha * Imake-sha * Yamagami-sha * Hakken-sha * Inari-sha * Tsushima-sha === Local information === ; Location * 488-0840 {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}Inba Motomachi 5-3-1 ; Access * 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Seto Line|Q6810422|WD=Q6810422}}{{ill|lt=Inba Station|Q8192040|WD=Q8192040}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.696 }} === References === * ''Owariasahi City History, Cultural Properties Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980 * ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 2011 === External links === * [https://sbkw.o.oo7.jp/index2.html Official website] {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しふかわしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:尾張旭市の歴史]] [[Category:尾張旭市の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

[[ファイル:Shibukawajinja.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}{{ill|lt=Inba Motomachi|Q86729809|WD=Q86729809}}5-3-1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|lt=Junior Third Rank|Q11071123|WD=Q11071123}} upper
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}
former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名 = Shibukawa Tenjin
Sobukawa Tenjin
Inba Tenjin
Tenjin |札所等 = |例祭 = Sunday nearest October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Shibukawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Shibukawa Shrine''' (しぶかわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Inba Motomachi|Q86729809|WD=Q86729809}}, {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} identified with "Shibukawa Shrine" of Yamada District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506}}, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}. In earlier times, "Shibukawa" was read "Sobukawa," and it is said to have attracted faith as the {{ill|lt=sōsha|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129}} of Yamada District, Owari Province. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} (たかみむすびのかみ) === History === Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q184963}}, it was called '''Tenjin'''. The 17th-century ''Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki'' records "Inba Village... Sobukawa Tenjin... exempt from levies from before," showing that it was already called "Sobukawa Tenjin" in the early Edo period. According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723}}, and in Hakuhō 5 ({{ill|lt=676|Q31098|WD=Q31098}}) it became the {{ill|lt=saijō|Q12042092|WD=Q12042092}} (yuniwa) for the {{ill|lt=Daijōsai|Q11433653|WD=Q11433653}} held in Yamada District, Owari Province. At founding, it is thought to have been at a site southwest of the current location called "Shibuko" (sobuko). The {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}} does mention that Yamada District in Owari was chosen as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yuki|1=UNKNOWN}} district for the {{ill|lt=Niinamesai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518}} in 676, but does not mention Shibukawa Shrine. It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030}} with "Shibukawa Shrine" in Yamada District, Owari, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and this became the accepted theory. Because the shrine and place names are similar, there has been almost no dissent from identifying this as Shibukawa Shrine, with only {{ill|lt=Tsuda Masao|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474}} raising doubts. For divine rank ({{ill|lt=shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}}), the Teiji manuscript of the {{ill|lt=Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830}} lists "Junior Third Rank Upper, Shibukawa Tenjin," while the Genki manuscript has "Junior Third Rank, Shibukawa Tenjin." In the Sengoku period, the shrine buildings were renovated by {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411}}. In Jōkyō 5 (1688), they were rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245}}, lord of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072}}. Major repairs were carried out in Kansei 3 (1791) and in 1898 (Meiji 31). In September 1959 (Shōwa 34), the {{ill|lt=Isewan Typhoon|Q1060156|WD=Q1060156}} toppled dozens of giant cypress trees over 300 years old. After that, roofs were rethatched every 33 to 34 years and shrine buildings restored every 100 years. In {{ill|lt=1897|Q7847|WD=Q7847}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 30), nearby Hakken-sha and Yamagami were merged into this shrine. Because these shrine structures were the oldest buildings in {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}, the city took a positive stance toward cultural-property designation and consulted its cultural property protection council. A survey by Chubu University engineering professor {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ono Takehisa|1=UNKNOWN}} was scheduled for May 12, 2002, but on May 4, eight days before the survey, a suspicious fire burned the shrine buildings to the ground. A temporary shrine was built in June that year, and the Shibukawa Shrine Reconstruction Committee was formed to plan remains surveys and rebuilding''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.694. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in May 2009 (Heisei 21). Construction cost exceeded 240 million yen, and more than 85 million yen in donations were collected. === Precincts === ; Buildings * Honden - {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}, cypress-bark roofing * Haiden * Temizuya * Assembly hall * Yuki-saiden monument ; Subsidiary shrines * East Gosha-no-miya * West Gosha-no-miya * Shinmei-sha * Kumano-sha * Hachiman-sha * Imake-sha * Yamagami-sha * Hakken-sha * Inari-sha * Tsushima-sha === Local information === ; Location * 488-0840 {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893}}Inba Motomachi 5-3-1 ; Access * 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Seto Line|Q6810422|WD=Q6810422}}{{ill|lt=Inba Station|Q8192040|WD=Q8192040}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.696 }} === References === * ''Owariasahi City History, Cultural Properties Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980 * ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 2011 === External links === * [https://sbkw.o.oo7.jp/index2.html Official website] {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しふかわしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:尾張旭市の歴史]] [[Category:尾張旭市の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |別名 = Shibukawa Tenjin
Sobukawa Tenjin
Inba Tenjin
Tenjin |札所等 = |例祭 = Sunday nearest October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Shibukawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Shibukawa Shrine''' (しぶかわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Inba Motomachi|Q86729809|WD=Q86729809|1=Inba Motomachi}}, {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} identified with "Shibukawa Shrine" of Yamada District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506|1=Owari Province}}, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. In earlier times, "Shibukawa" was read "Sobukawa," and it is said to have attracted faith as the {{ill|lt=sōsha|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129|1=Sōja shrine}} of Yamada District, Owari Province. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|1=Takamimusubi}} (たかみむすびのかみ) === History === Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period|WD=Q184963}}, it was called '''Tenjin'''. The 17th-century ''Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki'' records "Inba Village... Sobukawa Tenjin... exempt from levies from before," showing that it was already called "Sobukawa Tenjin" in the early Edo period. According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, and in Hakuhō 5 ({{ill|lt=676|Q31098|WD=Q31098|1=676}}) it became the {{ill|lt=saijō|Q12042092|WD=Q12042092|1=funeral hall}} (yuniwa) for the {{ill|lt=Daijōsai|Q11433653|WD=Q11433653|1=Daijosai}} held in Yamada District, Owari Province. At founding, it is thought to have been at a site southwest of the current location called "Shibuko" (sobuko). The {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}} does mention that Yamada District in Owari was chosen as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yuki|1=UNKNOWN}} district for the {{ill|lt=Niinamesai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}} in 676, but does not mention Shibukawa Shrine. It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030|1=Amano Sadakage}} with "Shibukawa Shrine" in Yamada District, Owari, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and this became the accepted theory. Because the shrine and place names are similar, there has been almost no dissent from identifying this as Shibukawa Shrine, with only {{ill|lt=Tsuda Masao|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474|1=Tsuda Masanari}} raising doubts. For divine rank ({{ill|lt=shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}), the Teiji manuscript of the {{ill|lt=Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830|1=Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō}} lists "Junior Third Rank Upper, Shibukawa Tenjin," while the Genki manuscript has "Junior Third Rank, Shibukawa Tenjin." In the Sengoku period, the shrine buildings were renovated by {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411|1=Oda Nobunaga}}. In Jōkyō 5 (1688), they were rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245|1=Tokugawa Mitsutomo}}, lord of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}. Major repairs were carried out in Kansei 3 (1791) and in 1898 (Meiji 31). In September 1959 (Shōwa 34), the {{ill|lt=Isewan Typhoon|Q1060156|WD=Q1060156|1=Typhoon Vera}} toppled dozens of giant cypress trees over 300 years old. After that, roofs were rethatched every 33 to 34 years and shrine buildings restored every 100 years. In {{ill|lt=1897|Q7847|WD=Q7847|1=1897}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 30), nearby Hakken-sha and Yamagami were merged into this shrine. Because these shrine structures were the oldest buildings in {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}, the city took a positive stance toward cultural-property designation and consulted its cultural property protection council. A survey by Chubu University engineering professor {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ono Takehisa|1=UNKNOWN}} was scheduled for May 12, 2002, but on May 4, eight days before the survey, a suspicious fire burned the shrine buildings to the ground. A temporary shrine was built in June that year, and the Shibukawa Shrine Reconstruction Committee was formed to plan remains surveys and rebuilding''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.694. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in May 2009 (Heisei 21). Construction cost exceeded 240 million yen, and more than 85 million yen in donations were collected. === Precincts === ; Buildings * Honden - {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, cypress-bark roofing * Haiden * Temizuya * Assembly hall * Yuki-saiden monument ; Subsidiary shrines * East Gosha-no-miya * West Gosha-no-miya * Shinmei-sha * Kumano-sha * Hachiman-sha * Imake-sha * Yamagami-sha * Hakken-sha * Inari-sha * Tsushima-sha === Local information === ; Location * 488-0840 {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}Inba Motomachi 5-3-1 ; Access * 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Seto Line|Q6810422|WD=Q6810422|1=Meitetsu Seto Line}}{{ill|lt=Inba Station|Q8192040|WD=Q8192040|1=Inba Station}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.696 }} === References === * ''Owariasahi City History, Cultural Properties Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980 * ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 2011 === External links === * [https://sbkw.o.oo7.jp/index2.html Official website] {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しふかわしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:尾張旭市の歴史]] [[Category:尾張旭市の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |別名 = Shibukawa Tenjin
Sobukawa Tenjin
Inba Tenjin
Tenjin |札所等 = |例祭 = Sunday nearest October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Shibukawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Shibukawa Shrine''' (しぶかわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Inba Motomachi|Q86729809|WD=Q86729809|1=Inba Motomachi}}, {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} identified with "Shibukawa Shrine" of Yamada District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506|1=Owari Province}}, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. In earlier times, "Shibukawa" was read "Sobukawa," and it is said to have attracted faith as the {{ill|lt=sōsha|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129|1=Sōja shrine}} of Yamada District, Owari Province. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|1=Takamimusubi}} (たかみむすびのかみ) === History === Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period|WD=Q184963}}, it was called '''Tenjin'''. The 17th-century ''Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki'' records "Inba Village... Sobukawa Tenjin... exempt from levies from before," showing that it was already called "Sobukawa Tenjin" in the early Edo period. According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, and in Hakuhō 5 ({{ill|lt=676|Q31098|WD=Q31098|1=676}}) it became the {{ill|lt=saijō|Q12042092|WD=Q12042092|1=funeral hall}} (yuniwa) for the {{ill|lt=Daijōsai|Q11433653|WD=Q11433653|1=Daijosai}} held in Yamada District, Owari Province. At founding, it is thought to have been at a site southwest of the current location called "Shibuko" (sobuko). The {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}} does mention that Yamada District in Owari was chosen as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yuki|1=UNKNOWN}} district for the {{ill|lt=Niinamesai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}} in 676, but does not mention Shibukawa Shrine. It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030|1=Amano Sadakage}} with "Shibukawa Shrine" in Yamada District, Owari, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and this became the accepted theory. Because the shrine and place names are similar, there has been almost no dissent from identifying this as Shibukawa Shrine, with only {{ill|lt=Tsuda Masao|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474|1=Tsuda Masanari}} raising doubts. For divine rank ({{ill|lt=shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}), the Teiji manuscript of the {{ill|lt=Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830|1=Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō}} lists "Junior Third Rank Upper, Shibukawa Tenjin," while the Genki manuscript has "Junior Third Rank, Shibukawa Tenjin." In the Sengoku period, the shrine buildings were renovated by {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411|1=Oda Nobunaga}}. In Jōkyō 5 (1688), they were rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245|1=Tokugawa Mitsutomo}}, lord of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}. Major repairs were carried out in Kansei 3 (1791) and in 1898 (Meiji 31). In September 1959 (Shōwa 34), the {{ill|lt=Isewan Typhoon|Q1060156|WD=Q1060156|1=Typhoon Vera}} toppled dozens of giant cypress trees over 300 years old. After that, roofs were rethatched every 33 to 34 years and shrine buildings restored every 100 years. In {{ill|lt=1897|Q7847|WD=Q7847|1=1897}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 30), nearby Hakken-sha and Yamagami were merged into this shrine. Because these shrine structures were the oldest buildings in {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}, the city took a positive stance toward cultural-property designation and consulted its cultural property protection council. A survey by Chubu University engineering professor {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ono Takehisa|1=UNKNOWN}} was scheduled for May 12, 2002, but on May 4, eight days before the survey, a suspicious fire burned the shrine buildings to the ground. A temporary shrine was built in June that year, and the Shibukawa Shrine Reconstruction Committee was formed to plan remains surveys and rebuilding''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.694. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in May 2009 (Heisei 21). Construction cost exceeded 240 million yen, and more than 85 million yen in donations were collected. === Precincts === ; Buildings * Honden - {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, cypress-bark roofing * Haiden * Temizuya * Assembly hall * Yuki-saiden monument ; Subsidiary shrines * East Gosha-no-miya * West Gosha-no-miya * Shinmei-sha * Kumano-sha * Hachiman-sha * Imake-sha * Yamagami-sha * Hakken-sha * Inari-sha * Tsushima-sha === Local information === ; Location * 488-0840 {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}Inba Motomachi 5-3-1 ; Access * 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Seto Line|Q6810422|WD=Q6810422|1=Meitetsu Seto Line}}{{ill|lt=Inba Station|Q8192040|WD=Q8192040|1=Inba Station}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.696 }} === References === * ''Owariasahi City History, Cultural Properties Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980 * ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 2011 === External links === * [https://sbkw.o.oo7.jp/index2.html Official website] {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しふかわしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:尾張旭市の歴史]] [[Category:尾張旭市の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |別名 = Shibukawa Tenjin
Sobukawa Tenjin
Inba Tenjin
Tenjin |札所等 = |例祭 = Sunday nearest October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Shibukawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Shibukawa Shrine''' (しぶかわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Inba Motomachi|Q86729809|WD=Q86729809|1=Inba Motomachi}}, {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} identified with "Shibukawa Shrine" of Yamada District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506|1=Owari Province}}, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. In earlier times, "Shibukawa" was read "Sobukawa," and it is said to have attracted faith as the {{ill|lt=sōsha|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129|1=Sōja shrine}} of Yamada District, Owari Province. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|1=Takamimusubi}} (たかみむすびのかみ) === History === Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period|WD=Q184963}}, it was called '''Tenjin'''. The 17th-century ''Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki'' records "Inba Village... Sobukawa Tenjin... exempt from levies from before," showing that it was already called "Sobukawa Tenjin" in the early Edo period. According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, and in Hakuhō 5 ({{ill|lt=676|Q31098|WD=Q31098|1=676}}) it became the {{ill|lt=saijō|Q12042092|WD=Q12042092|1=funeral hall}} (yuniwa) for the {{ill|lt=Daijōsai|Q11433653|WD=Q11433653|1=Daijosai}} held in Yamada District, Owari Province. At founding, it is thought to have been at a site southwest of the current location called "Shibuko" (sobuko). The {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}} does mention that Yamada District in Owari was chosen as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yuki|1=UNKNOWN}} district for the {{ill|lt=Niinamesai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}} in 676, but does not mention Shibukawa Shrine. It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030|1=Amano Sadakage}} with "Shibukawa Shrine" in Yamada District, Owari, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and this became the accepted theory. Because the shrine and place names are similar, there has been almost no dissent from identifying this as Shibukawa Shrine, with only {{ill|lt=Tsuda Masao|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474|1=Tsuda Masanari}} raising doubts. For divine rank ({{ill|lt=shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}), the Teiji manuscript of the {{ill|lt=Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830|1=Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō}} lists "Junior Third Rank Upper, Shibukawa Tenjin," while the Genki manuscript has "Junior Third Rank, Shibukawa Tenjin." In the Sengoku period, the shrine buildings were renovated by {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411|1=Oda Nobunaga}}. In Jōkyō 5 (1688), they were rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245|1=Tokugawa Mitsutomo}}, lord of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}. Major repairs were carried out in Kansei 3 (1791) and in 1898 (Meiji 31). In September 1959 (Shōwa 34), the {{ill|lt=Isewan Typhoon|Q1060156|WD=Q1060156|1=Typhoon Vera}} toppled dozens of giant cypress trees over 300 years old. After that, roofs were rethatched every 33 to 34 years and shrine buildings restored every 100 years. In {{ill|lt=1897|Q7847|WD=Q7847|1=1897}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 30), nearby Hakken-sha and Yamagami were merged into this shrine. Because these shrine structures were the oldest buildings in {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}, the city took a positive stance toward cultural-property designation and consulted its cultural property protection council. A survey by Chubu University engineering professor {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ono Takehisa|1=UNKNOWN}} was scheduled for May 12, 2002, but on May 4, eight days before the survey, a suspicious fire burned the shrine buildings to the ground. A temporary shrine was built in June that year, and the Shibukawa Shrine Reconstruction Committee was formed to plan remains surveys and rebuilding''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.694. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in May 2009 (Heisei 21). Construction cost exceeded 240 million yen, and more than 85 million yen in donations were collected. === Precincts === ; Buildings * Honden - {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, cypress-bark roofing * Haiden * Temizuya * Assembly hall * Yuki-saiden monument ; Subsidiary shrines * East Gosha-no-miya * West Gosha-no-miya * Shinmei-sha * Kumano-sha * Hachiman-sha * Imake-sha * Yamagami-sha * Hakken-sha * Inari-sha * Tsushima-sha === Local information === ; Location * 488-0840 {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}Inba Motomachi 5-3-1 ; Access * 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Seto Line|Q6810422|WD=Q6810422|1=Meitetsu Seto Line}}{{ill|lt=Inba Station|Q8192040|WD=Q8192040|1=Inba Station}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.696 }} === References === * ''Owariasahi City History, Cultural Properties Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980 * ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 2011 === External links === * [https://sbkw.o.oo7.jp/index2.html Official website] {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しふかわしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:尾張旭市の歴史]] [[Category:尾張旭市の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Shibukawa Shrine |画像 = [[ファイル:Shibukawajinja.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}{{ill|lt=Inba Motomachi|Q86729809|WD=Q86729809|1=Inba Motomachi}}5-3-1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|1=Takamimusubi}} |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|lt=Junior Third Rank|Q11071123|WD=Q11071123|1=Junior Third Rank}} upper
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}
former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |別名 = Shibukawa Tenjin
Sobukawa Tenjin
Inba Tenjin
Tenjin |札所等 = |例祭 = Sunday nearest October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Shibukawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Shibukawa Shrine''' (しぶかわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Inba Motomachi|Q86729809|WD=Q86729809|1=Inba Motomachi}}, {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} identified with "Shibukawa Shrine" of Yamada District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506|1=Owari Province}}, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. In earlier times, "Shibukawa" was read "Sobukawa," and it is said to have attracted faith as the {{ill|lt=sōsha|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129|1=Sōja shrine}} of Yamada District, Owari Province. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|1=Takamimusubi}} (たかみむすびのかみ) === History === Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period|WD=Q184963}}, it was called '''Tenjin'''. The 17th-century ''Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki'' records "Inba Village... Sobukawa Tenjin... exempt from levies from before," showing that it was already called "Sobukawa Tenjin" in the early Edo period. According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, and in Hakuhō 5 ({{ill|lt=676|Q31098|WD=Q31098|1=676}}) it became the {{ill|lt=saijō|Q12042092|WD=Q12042092|1=funeral hall}} (yuniwa) for the {{ill|lt=Daijōsai|Q11433653|WD=Q11433653|1=Daijosai}} held in Yamada District, Owari Province. At founding, it is thought to have been at a site southwest of the current location called "Shibuko" (sobuko). The {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}} does mention that Yamada District in Owari was chosen as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yuki|1=UNKNOWN}} district for the {{ill|lt=Niinamesai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}} in 676, but does not mention Shibukawa Shrine. It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030|1=Amano Sadakage}} with "Shibukawa Shrine" in Yamada District, Owari, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and this became the accepted theory. Because the shrine and place names are similar, there has been almost no dissent from identifying this as Shibukawa Shrine, with only {{ill|lt=Tsuda Masao|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474|1=Tsuda Masanari}} raising doubts. For divine rank ({{ill|lt=shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}), the Teiji manuscript of the {{ill|lt=Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830|1=Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō}} lists "Junior Third Rank Upper, Shibukawa Tenjin," while the Genki manuscript has "Junior Third Rank, Shibukawa Tenjin." In the Sengoku period, the shrine buildings were renovated by {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411|1=Oda Nobunaga}}. In Jōkyō 5 (1688), they were rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245|1=Tokugawa Mitsutomo}}, lord of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}. Major repairs were carried out in Kansei 3 (1791) and in 1898 (Meiji 31). In September 1959 (Shōwa 34), the {{ill|lt=Isewan Typhoon|Q1060156|WD=Q1060156|1=Typhoon Vera}} toppled dozens of giant cypress trees over 300 years old. After that, roofs were rethatched every 33 to 34 years and shrine buildings restored every 100 years. In {{ill|lt=1897|Q7847|WD=Q7847|1=1897}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 30), nearby Hakken-sha and Yamagami were merged into this shrine. Because these shrine structures were the oldest buildings in {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}, the city took a positive stance toward cultural-property designation and consulted its cultural property protection council. A survey by Chubu University engineering professor {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ono Takehisa|1=UNKNOWN}} was scheduled for May 12, 2002, but on May 4, eight days before the survey, a suspicious fire burned the shrine buildings to the ground. A temporary shrine was built in June that year, and the Shibukawa Shrine Reconstruction Committee was formed to plan remains surveys and rebuilding''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.694. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in May 2009 (Heisei 21). Construction cost exceeded 240 million yen, and more than 85 million yen in donations were collected. === Precincts === ; Buildings * Honden - {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, cypress-bark roofing * Haiden * Temizuya * Assembly hall * Yuki-saiden monument ; Subsidiary shrines * East Gosha-no-miya * West Gosha-no-miya * Shinmei-sha * Kumano-sha * Hachiman-sha * Imake-sha * Yamagami-sha * Hakken-sha * Inari-sha * Tsushima-sha === Local information === ; Location * 488-0840 {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}Inba Motomachi 5-3-1 ; Access * 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Seto Line|Q6810422|WD=Q6810422|1=Meitetsu Seto Line}}{{ill|lt=Inba Station|Q8192040|WD=Q8192040|1=Inba Station}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.696 }} === References === * ''Owariasahi City History, Cultural Properties Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980 * ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 2011 === External links === * [https://sbkw.o.oo7.jp/index2.html Official website] {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しふかわしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:尾張旭市の歴史]] [[Category:尾張旭市の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QShibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)}}[[Category:qq]]

Shibukawa Shrine |画像 = [[ファイル:Shibukawajinja.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}{{ill|lt=Inba Motomachi|Q86729809|WD=Q86729809|1=Inba Motomachi}}5-3-1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|1=Takamimusubi}} |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|lt=Junior Third Rank|Q11071123|WD=Q11071123|1=Junior Third Rank}} upper
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}
former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |別名 = Shibukawa Tenjin
Sobukawa Tenjin
Inba Tenjin
Tenjin |札所等 = |例祭 = Sunday nearest October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Shibukawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Shibukawa Shrine''' (しぶかわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Inba Motomachi|Q86729809|WD=Q86729809|1=Inba Motomachi}}, {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} identified with "Shibukawa Shrine" of Yamada District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506|1=Owari Province}}, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. In earlier times, "Shibukawa" was read "Sobukawa," and it is said to have attracted faith as the {{ill|lt=sōsha|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129|1=Sōja shrine}} of Yamada District, Owari Province. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|1=Takamimusubi}} (たかみむすびのかみ) === History === Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period|WD=Q184963}}, it was called '''Tenjin'''. The 17th-century ''Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki'' records "Inba Village... Sobukawa Tenjin... exempt from levies from before," showing that it was already called "Sobukawa Tenjin" in the early Edo period. According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, and in Hakuhō 5 ({{ill|lt=676|Q31098|WD=Q31098|1=676}}) it became the {{ill|lt=saijō|Q12042092|WD=Q12042092|1=funeral hall}} (yuniwa) for the {{ill|lt=Daijōsai|Q11433653|WD=Q11433653|1=Daijosai}} held in Yamada District, Owari Province. At founding, it is thought to have been at a site southwest of the current location called "Shibuko" (sobuko). The {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}} does mention that Yamada District in Owari was chosen as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yuki|1=UNKNOWN}} district for the {{ill|lt=Niinamesai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}} in 676, but does not mention Shibukawa Shrine. It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030|1=Amano Sadakage}} with "Shibukawa Shrine" in Yamada District, Owari, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and this became the accepted theory. Because the shrine and place names are similar, there has been almost no dissent from identifying this as Shibukawa Shrine, with only {{ill|lt=Tsuda Masao|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474|1=Tsuda Masanari}} raising doubts. For divine rank ({{ill|lt=shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}), the Teiji manuscript of the {{ill|lt=Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830|1=Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō}} lists "Junior Third Rank Upper, Shibukawa Tenjin," while the Genki manuscript has "Junior Third Rank, Shibukawa Tenjin." In the Sengoku period, the shrine buildings were renovated by {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411|1=Oda Nobunaga}}. In Jōkyō 5 (1688), they were rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245|1=Tokugawa Mitsutomo}}, lord of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}. Major repairs were carried out in Kansei 3 (1791) and in 1898 (Meiji 31). In September 1959 (Shōwa 34), the {{ill|lt=Isewan Typhoon|Q1060156|WD=Q1060156|1=Typhoon Vera}} toppled dozens of giant cypress trees over 300 years old. After that, roofs were rethatched every 33 to 34 years and shrine buildings restored every 100 years. In {{ill|lt=1897|Q7847|WD=Q7847|1=1897}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 30), nearby Hakken-sha and Yamagami were merged into this shrine. Because these shrine structures were the oldest buildings in {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}, the city took a positive stance toward cultural-property designation and consulted its cultural property protection council. A survey by Chubu University engineering professor {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ono Takehisa|1=UNKNOWN}} was scheduled for May 12, 2002, but on May 4, eight days before the survey, a suspicious fire burned the shrine buildings to the ground. A temporary shrine was built in June that year, and the Shibukawa Shrine Reconstruction Committee was formed to plan remains surveys and rebuilding''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.694. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in May 2009 (Heisei 21). Construction cost exceeded 240 million yen, and more than 85 million yen in donations were collected. === Precincts === ; Buildings * Honden - {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, cypress-bark roofing * Haiden * Temizuya * Assembly hall * Yuki-saiden monument ; Subsidiary shrines * East Gosha-no-miya * West Gosha-no-miya * Shinmei-sha * Kumano-sha * Hachiman-sha * Imake-sha * Yamagami-sha * Hakken-sha * Inari-sha * Tsushima-sha === Local information === ; Location * 488-0840 {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}Inba Motomachi 5-3-1 ; Access * 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Seto Line|Q6810422|WD=Q6810422|1=Meitetsu Seto Line}}{{ill|lt=Inba Station|Q8192040|WD=Q8192040|1=Inba Station}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.696 }} === References === * ''Owariasahi City History, Cultural Properties Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980 * ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 2011 === External links === * [https://sbkw.o.oo7.jp/index2.html Official website] {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:尾張旭市の歴史]] [[Category:尾張旭市の建築物]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社]] [[Category:qq]]

Shibukawa Shrine |画像 = [[ファイル:Shibukawajinja.JPG|270px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}{{ill|lt=Inba Motomachi|Q86729809|WD=Q86729809|1=Inba Motomachi}}5-3-1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|1=Takamimusubi}} |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|lt=Junior Third Rank|Q11071123|WD=Q11071123|1=Junior Third Rank}} upper
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}
former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |別名 = Shibukawa Tenjin
Sobukawa Tenjin
Inba Tenjin
Tenjin |札所等 = |例祭 = Sunday nearest October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Shibukawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Shibukawa Shrine''' (しぶかわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Inba Motomachi|Q86729809|WD=Q86729809|1=Inba Motomachi}}, {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} identified with "Shibukawa Shrine" of Yamada District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506|1=Owari Province}}, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. In earlier times, "Shibukawa" was read "Sobukawa," and it is said to have attracted faith as the {{ill|lt=sōsha|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129|1=Sōja shrine}} of Yamada District, Owari Province. === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|1=Takamimusubi}} (たかみむすびのかみ) === History === Until the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period|WD=Q184963}}, it was called '''Tenjin'''. The 17th-century ''Kanbun Muramura Oboegaki'' records "Inba Village... Sobukawa Tenjin... exempt from levies from before," showing that it was already called "Sobukawa Tenjin" in the early Edo period. According to shrine tradition, it was founded in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, and in Hakuhō 5 ({{ill|lt=676|Q31098|WD=Q31098|1=676}}) it became the {{ill|lt=saijō|Q12042092|WD=Q12042092|1=funeral hall}} (yuniwa) for the {{ill|lt=Daijōsai|Q11433653|WD=Q11433653|1=Daijosai}} held in Yamada District, Owari Province. At founding, it is thought to have been at a site southwest of the current location called "Shibuko" (sobuko). The {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}} does mention that Yamada District in Owari was chosen as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yuki|1=UNKNOWN}} district for the {{ill|lt=Niinamesai|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}} in 676, but does not mention Shibukawa Shrine. It was identified by {{ill|lt=Amano Nobukage|Q6535030|WD=Q6535030|1=Amano Sadakage}} with "Shibukawa Shrine" in Yamada District, Owari, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and this became the accepted theory. Because the shrine and place names are similar, there has been almost no dissent from identifying this as Shibukawa Shrine, with only {{ill|lt=Tsuda Masao|Q22123474|WD=Q22123474|1=Tsuda Masanari}} raising doubts. For divine rank ({{ill|lt=shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}), the Teiji manuscript of the {{ill|lt=Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11041830|WD=Q11041830|1=Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō}} lists "Junior Third Rank Upper, Shibukawa Tenjin," while the Genki manuscript has "Junior Third Rank, Shibukawa Tenjin." In the Sengoku period, the shrine buildings were renovated by {{ill|lt=Oda Nobunaga|Q171411|WD=Q171411|1=Oda Nobunaga}}. In Jōkyō 5 (1688), they were rebuilt by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsutomo|Q843245|WD=Q843245|1=Tokugawa Mitsutomo}}, lord of the {{ill|lt=Owari Domain|Q1062072|WD=Q1062072|1=Owari Domain}}. Major repairs were carried out in Kansei 3 (1791) and in 1898 (Meiji 31). In September 1959 (Shōwa 34), the {{ill|lt=Isewan Typhoon|Q1060156|WD=Q1060156|1=Typhoon Vera}} toppled dozens of giant cypress trees over 300 years old. After that, roofs were rethatched every 33 to 34 years and shrine buildings restored every 100 years. In {{ill|lt=1897|Q7847|WD=Q7847|1=1897}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 30), nearby Hakken-sha and Yamagami were merged into this shrine. Because these shrine structures were the oldest buildings in {{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}, the city took a positive stance toward cultural-property designation and consulted its cultural property protection council. A survey by Chubu University engineering professor {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ono Takehisa|1=UNKNOWN}} was scheduled for May 12, 2002, but on May 4, eight days before the survey, a suspicious fire burned the shrine buildings to the ground. A temporary shrine was built in June that year, and the Shibukawa Shrine Reconstruction Committee was formed to plan remains surveys and rebuilding''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.694. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in May 2009 (Heisei 21). Construction cost exceeded 240 million yen, and more than 85 million yen in donations were collected. === Precincts === ; Buildings * Honden - {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, cypress-bark roofing * Haiden * Temizuya * Assembly hall * Yuki-saiden monument ; Subsidiary shrines * East Gosha-no-miya * West Gosha-no-miya * Shinmei-sha * Kumano-sha * Hachiman-sha * Imake-sha * Yamagami-sha * Hakken-sha * Inari-sha * Tsushima-sha === Local information === ; Location * 488-0840 {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Owariasahi|Q694893|WD=Q694893|1=Owariasahi}}Inba Motomachi 5-3-1 ; Access * 5 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Seto Line|Q6810422|WD=Q6810422|1=Meitetsu Seto Line}}{{ill|lt=Inba Station|Q8192040|WD=Q8192040|1=Inba Station}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980, p.696 }} === References === * ''Owariasahi City History, Cultural Properties Volume'', Owariasahi City, 1980 * ''Owariasahi City History, Modern History Volume'', Owariasahi City, 2011 === External links === * [https://sbkw.o.oo7.jp/index2.html Official website] {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Shibukawa Shrine (Q100544140)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:尾張旭市の歴史]] [[Category:尾張旭市の建築物]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:qq]]
